Hey guys, JB here, time for your daily motivation slash skills training. I love this one. Ready for this? The judging the book by its cover story, right? You went through the training now, you're in, I think it was lesson two, I think, or one, whatever the matter. Point is, right, is that, you know, I was always told by my parents, don't judge a book by its cover, right? You know, they did so well. I'll give you a perfect example. I flew to New York this weekend, right? And my mom was talking about something, you know, back in the old days, my dad's sick, you know, he's getting better thank God, he's in the hospital, right? And my mom was, you know, reminiscing about, you know, back in the old days, she'd go into work, was driven by this guy who was on next door neighbor, his name is Lenny Lipsky, right? And she was, oh, you know, she liked it, but Lenny was all flash. She was just a marketing guy, no substance behind that. And I'm like, mom, I'm seeing, honestly, I'm like, you know, why would you say that? You know, what's that? That judgment about? And she's like, no, it's just the way he carries himself. Now I know Lenny's passed away, right? But the fact is Lenny was like a sharp dresser. Okay. He just carried himself a certain way that in my mother's mind, it just, you know, it all corralled up into Lenny was a sharpie. Lenny was a guy on the make. Lenny was a more, he's more about flash than substance. And I knew Lenny, it wasn't true. Just remember the way that you move through the world, the appearance, the air that you give off the people is so crucial in those first few seconds and the moments thereafter. You know, my mother would be the first one to say, don't judge a book by its cover and her whole life she does it. Not that she's, you know, evil. She's not a bad person. She's a good person. We all do it. We all wire that way. It's an instinctive thing that we size up us around him instantly. And then based on what we size up, we say, well, should we, you know, what should we do? Should we stay or run? So let's fight a flight. But in modern times, it's, you know, is there benefits here to stick around and listen to? Or am I just out before it starts? You give off that wrong air. You come off it and this is one thing that a lot of salespeople, I don't know your account and speech to a bunch of people. Right. But I don't know what's, what's your gig? You know, what do you do? How do you come off? Do you come off like a slick salesperson? That's not what the straight line is all about. You have to be the opposite. The ethics and the integrity. The, you know, it's just going to ooze out of you. You know, when I'm speaking fast right now, it's because I'm trying to train him, getting a lot of words and when I'm actually selling somebody, I'm speaking slowly. I'm on par with them. Meaning I match my rate of speech to their rate of speech. I'm getting into rapport people on a very deep level. And I'll tell you this, the number one way I'm jumping at a bit of the number one way you really get that right appearance out there is by asking questions. It's those questions that you ask and the way that you listen using your body language and your tone. I'm, that's what connects people. I want you to spend this week mastering the art of active listening. You get, I want you to really focus. Don't ever, you know, it's funny my son, Bo, I'm very sharp, right? You don't have to say I'm sharp, okay? Bo used to not be a very good active listener because he would be thinking. He would listen to you and he was thinking. You look up into space and he was completely listening. He was hanging on your every word, but you didn't know that. So I'm like, are you listening to me? He's like, yeah, I'm thinking what you said. And I really sort of busted his balls about that. I said, Bo, and when people talk to you through your, huh, mm, mm, mm, mm, got it. People don't know that you understand that you get them. You do that to those little drunks and groans. Once you start doing that, it almost, it's like the amplifier of all things rapport driven. Seriously, when people know that you're listening and when I say that you care, like, mm, mm, I, I'm in it versus, mm, mm, someone talks to you like, and your mind's going a mile a minute. How did they know? For all they know, you're just tuned out and you don't give a crap. You have to give active feedback when you're on the phone and you ask a question to someone and they start, uh-huh, mm, mm, mm, mm, mm, mm, mm, mm. Oh, yep, huh, yep, got it.
